draft.introduction: The parable of the rich man and Lazarus, unique to Luke. It provides the most detailed picture of the afterlife in the Gospels: Abraham’s bosom, a great chasm, and torment in flames.

translation.text: There was a rich man who was clothed in purple and fine linen and who feasted sumptuously every day. And at his gate was laid a poor man named Lazarus, covered with sores. The poor man died and was carried by the angels to Abraham’s side. The rich man also died and was buried, and in Hades, being in torment, he lifted up his eyes and saw Abraham far off and Lazarus at his side. And Abraham said, “Between us and you a great chasm has been fixed.”
translation.original_text: μεταξὺ ἡμῶν καὶ ὑμῶν χάσμα μέγα ἐστήρικται

translator_notes.note: The Greek ὅδης (Hades) is the realm of the dead, not γέεννα (Gehenna, the place of final punishment). This parable describes an intermediate state between death and final judgment—a distinction that became important in both Eastern and Western theology.
